Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

David Chalmers

b. 1966

The hard problem, naturalistic dualism, and the structure of conscious experience.

This report treats David Chalmers not merely as the author of a famous slogan, “the hard problem of consciousness,” but as a systematic philosopher of mind whose work links metaphysics, philosophy of psychology, cognitive science, artificial intelligence, epistemology, and the methodology of consciousness science. The guiding thesis is that Chalmers’s philosophy of mind is a form of naturalistic anti-reductionism: it accepts the scientific study of cognition, computation, behavior, and neural mechanisms, but denies that such science, as currently conceived in purely physical-functional terms, can exhaustively explain conscious experience.

Chalmers’s project is best understood as a sustained attempt to extract a complete theory of mind from one central distinction: the distinction between mind as function and mind as experience. Psychology and cognitive science can explain the former by computational, neural, behavioral, and functional models. But phenomenal consciousness — what it is like to see red, feel pain, hear music, think vividly, or be an experiencing subject — requires additional principles. Those principles, for Chalmers, may still be natural laws; they are not supernatural. But they are not reducible to ordinary physical or functional laws.

Chalmers is currently University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science at New York University, and his work ranges across philosophy of mind, artificial intelligence, cognitive science, metaphysics, epistemology, language, physics, and technology. That range matters because his philosophy of mind is not isolated from his broader philosophical architecture: his work on two-dimensional semantics, scrutability, virtual reality, computation, and artificial intelligence all feed back into his account of mind.

1. Historical Position: Why Chalmers Matters in the Development of Philosophy of Mind

Chalmers enters the field after several major twentieth-century attempts to naturalize the mind. The broad historical sequence is roughly this: behaviorism tried to identify or analyze mental states in terms of behavior or behavioral dispositions; mind-brain identity theory identified mental states with brain states; functionalism then argued that mental states are individuated by their causal roles rather than by a specific biological substrate; computational cognitive science treated cognition as information-processing; and late twentieth-century consciousness studies reopened the question of whether these approaches could explain subjective experience.

Physicalism, in its contemporary sense, is the view that everything is physical or depends in the relevant way on the physical. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that “physicalism” and “materialism” are now often used interchangeably, although “physicalism” is tied more explicitly to the conception of the world given by physics. Modern identity theory emerged in the 1950s with figures such as U. T. Place, Herbert Feigl, and J. J. C. Smart, who proposed that mental states and processes are identical with brain states and processes. Functionalism then became the dominant philosophy of mind framework during the last third of the twentieth century, holding that mental states are defined by their causal or functional roles: their relations to stimuli, behavior, and other mental states.

Chalmers’s intervention is historically significant because it does not reject cognitive science, functionalism, or neuroscience wholesale. Instead, it argues that these traditions explain only one side of mind. They explain discrimination, report, attention, memory, integration, control, and other cognitive capacities. But they do not explain why those capacities are accompanied by subjective experience. In this sense, Chalmers is not a pre-scientific dualist returning to Cartesian metaphysics; he is a post-functionalist philosopher who accepts much of cognitive science but denies that functional explanation can close the explanatory gap.

His predecessors include Thomas Nagel, Frank Jackson, Joseph Levine, Saul Kripke, Bertrand Russell, C. D. Broad, and, more distantly, Leibniz and Descartes. Nagel’s “what it is like” formulation made subjectivity central; Jackson’s knowledge argument suggested that complete physical knowledge may leave out phenomenal knowledge; Levine’s “explanatory gap” named the residual gap between physical explanation and experience; Kripke’s modal arguments challenged type-identity theories; Broad and Russell provided older frameworks for nonreductive and Russellian approaches to mind. Chalmers systematizes these lines of thought into a rigorous late twentieth-century anti-reductionist program. The knowledge argument became especially influential after Jackson’s 1982 article, and Chalmers is one of the prominent philosophers who has used it in an explicitly anti-physicalist direction.

Chalmers’s 1995 paper “Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness” introduced the now-standard distinction between the “easy problems” and the “hard problem” of consciousness. His 1996 book The Conscious Mind then gave the distinction its full metaphysical and epistemological apparatus, arguing that reductive explanation of consciousness is impossible and that we must go beyond strict materialism by positing fundamental laws connecting physical processes and experience.

Historically, then, Chalmers occupies a precise position: he is one of the central figures in the revival of phenomenal consciousness as the core problem for philosophy of mind after the dominance of behaviorism, identity theory, functionalism, and computationalism. He does not oppose those traditions as theories of cognition. He opposes their claim to be complete theories of consciousness.

2. The Fundamental Distinction: Psychological Mind and Phenomenal Mind

The foundation of Chalmers’s philosophy of mind is a distinction between two concepts of mind. One concept is psychological or functional: mind as the system of capacities involved in perception, cognition, reasoning, attention, memory, action, language, and behavioral control. The other is phenomenal: mind as conscious experience, the felt or subjective character of mental life.

This distinction is already structurally visible in The Conscious Mind, whose opening chapters distinguish two concepts of mind and analyze supervenience, explanation, the phenomenal, and the psychological. The later chapters then argue for the irreducibility of consciousness, naturalistic dualism, organizational invariance, and the possibility of strong artificial intelligence.

The “easy problems” are not easy in practice. They are scientifically difficult. But they are “easy” in Chalmers’s technical sense because we can see what an explanation would look like: one specifies the mechanisms that perform the relevant function. Chalmers lists such problems as discrimination and categorization, integration of information, reportability, access to internal states, attention, deliberate behavioral control, and the distinction between wakefulness and sleep. These are problems about what organisms or systems do. They are vulnerable, in principle, to computational, cognitive, and neural explanation.

The hard problem is different. It asks why and how physical or functional processes are accompanied by experience at all. Even after we explain how information is processed, how a stimulus is categorized, how a report is produced, or how attention is controlled, there remains a further question: why should any of this be accompanied by subjective feeling? Chalmers explicitly frames the hard problem as the problem of experience, invoking Nagel’s idea of “what it is like” to be a conscious subject.

This distinction drives almost everything else in Chalmers’s work. It means that there are, in effect, two mind-body problems. The first is the relation between the body and psychological capacities: how the brain supports cognition, perception, memory, attention, language, agency, and behavior. The second is the relation between the body and phenomenal consciousness: why any physical or cognitive process should have a felt subjective aspect.

For Chalmers, contemporary cognitive science is very well suited to the first problem. It may eventually solve it in enormous detail. But it does not thereby solve the second. A complete philosophy of mind must therefore include both a science of cognitive function and a theory of phenomenal consciousness.

3. The Hard Problem: The Core of Chalmers’s Anti-Reductionism

Chalmers’s central negative claim is that ordinary reductive explanation cannot explain consciousness. Reductive explanation succeeds when we can analyze a phenomenon in terms of a function, mechanism, or structural role. Life, digestion, heat, memory retrieval, visual discrimination, and linguistic production can be explained by showing how underlying physical systems perform the relevant functions. But consciousness, as experience, is not simply a function to be performed.

The point is not that neuroscience is currently incomplete. Chalmers’s claim is stronger: even a complete account of neural mechanisms would still leave open why those mechanisms are accompanied by experience. The problem is structural. Functional explanation explains functions. But experience is not captured merely by specifying a function.

In “Facing Up,” Chalmers argues that theories such as Crick and Koch’s neural theory or Baars’s global workspace theory may explain important functional or cognitive phenomena — binding, accessibility, reportability, global availability — but they still leave open why those mechanisms should produce experience. The explanatory residue remains even when the functional story is sophisticated.

This is why Chalmers resists what he regards as deflationary solutions. Some theorists respond by saying that consciousness just is access, reportability, higher-order monitoring, global availability, integrated information, or certain neural dynamics. Chalmers’s reply is that such accounts may identify correlates, mechanisms, or functional roles, but they do not explain why there is something it is like to instantiate them. To reduce consciousness to a function is to change the subject from phenomenal consciousness to psychological consciousness.

The hard problem is therefore not merely a puzzle within neuroscience. It is a challenge to a whole explanatory paradigm: the assumption that all real phenomena can be explained by physical structure, physical dynamics, and functional organization alone.

4. Supervenience, Reductive Explanation, and the Failure of Physicalism

Chalmers formulates much of his case using the concept of supervenience. Roughly, one set of facts supervenes on another when there cannot be a difference in the first without a difference in the second. If mental facts metaphysically supervene on physical facts, then fixing all physical facts fixes all mental facts. If phenomenal facts do not supervene in this way, physicalism is false.

Chalmers’s crucial claim is not merely that we currently lack a derivation from physics to consciousness. It is that phenomenal truths are not a priori entailed by physical truths. In “Consciousness and its Place in Nature,” he frames the anti-materialist argument around the claim that physical accounts explain structure and function, while structure and function do not suffice for consciousness.

The argumentative structure is approximately this:

First, a complete physical description of the world gives us its physical structure and dynamics.

Second, from purely structural-dynamical information, we can derive only further structural-dynamical facts.

Third, facts about conscious experience are not merely structural-dynamical facts.

Therefore, consciousness is not entailed by the complete physical description.

In another formulation, if all physical truths do not necessitate all phenomenal truths, then physicalism is false. Chalmers explicitly argues that materialism requires phenomenal truths to be necessitated by physical truths; if they are not, then consciousness is not wholly physical in the relevant sense.

This is the philosophical engine of Chalmers’s anti-reductionism. It is not based on ignorance of neuroscience, nor on a mystical intuition that consciousness is spooky. It is based on the claim that physical-functional description leaves something out even when complete.

5. The Zombie Argument and the Conceivability Argument

The best-known version of Chalmers’s argument is the zombie argument. A philosophical zombie is a being physically and functionally identical to a conscious human being, but with no conscious experience. It behaves exactly as we do, reports experiences exactly as we do, and has the same neural and functional organization, but there is nothing it is like to be that being.

The zombie argument can be summarized as follows:
1. We can coherently conceive of a physical duplicate of our world in which there is no consciousness.
2. If such a world is ideally conceivable, then it is metaphysically possible, at least in the relevant sense.
3. If such a world is metaphysically possible, then consciousness does not metaphysically supervene on the physical.
4. If consciousness does not metaphysically supervene on the physical, then physicalism is false.

The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes zombies as creatures physically just like us but lacking conscious experience, and notes that if zombies are possible, physicalism is false or at least some form of dualism is true. Chalmers’s own “Consciousness and its Place in Nature” presents the conceivability argument in terms of the apparent coherence of physical duplicates without consciousness and connects that conceivability to the failure of physical necessitation.

The zombie argument is often misunderstood. Chalmers is not saying zombies are naturally possible, likely, or scientifically expected. He is not claiming that there are actual zombies. The claim is modal: if the physical facts can be held fixed while phenomenal facts vary, then phenomenal facts are not identical with, reducible to, or necessitated by physical facts.

The key controversial premise is the move from conceivability to possibility. Chalmers defends this through his work on two-dimensional semantics, which distinguishes different ways in which terms have intensions or meanings across possible scenarios. His two-dimensional argument is designed to block the standard physicalist reply that consciousness might be identical to a physical state in the same way that water is H₂O or heat is molecular motion. Chalmers argues that these scientific identities work because the relevant ordinary concepts have functional or structural criteria that can be satisfied by the physical story. Phenomenal concepts do not work that way. His consciousness papers describe the two-dimensional argument as his most developed version of the conceivability argument against materialism.

6. The Knowledge Argument, Phenomenal Concepts, and the Explanatory Gap

Chalmers also draws support from the family of arguments associated with Frank Jackson’s Mary case. Mary is a brilliant color scientist who knows all physical facts about color vision but has never seen red. When she first sees red, it seems that she learns something new: what red looks like. This suggests that complete physical knowledge does not entail complete phenomenal knowledge.

The knowledge argument became a central debate after Jackson’s 1982 paper, and it is traditionally understood as an argument against physicalism. Chalmers is one of the prominent philosophers who preserves its anti-physicalist force, even though Jackson himself later changed his mind.

Chalmers’s use of this argument is not merely epistemological. He treats the epistemic gap as evidence of an ontological gap. If complete physical knowledge leaves open phenomenal truths, then the physical truths do not fully determine the phenomenal truths. Physicalists often reply that Mary gains a new ability, a new concept, or a new mode of presentation rather than new factual knowledge. Chalmers’s response is that these replies may explain some features of the case, but they do not dissolve the basic gap between physical description and phenomenal reality.

This is where phenomenal concepts enter Chalmers’s system. Phenomenal concepts are concepts we use to think about experiences from the first-person perspective. They are not ordinary descriptive or functional concepts. Chalmers has written extensively on phenomenal concepts, phenomenal belief, and phenomenal knowledge, especially in connection with the explanatory gap.

The physicalist “phenomenal concept strategy” says that the explanatory gap arises not because consciousness is nonphysical, but because our concepts of consciousness are special. Chalmers’s reply is that this does not suffice. If phenomenal concepts are special in a way that cannot itself be physically explained, the gap remains. If they can be physically explained, we still need to explain why they refer to genuine phenomenal properties rather than merely to functional or neural states.

Thus Chalmers’s anti-reductionism has both metaphysical and epistemological dimensions. Phenomenal consciousness is not just hard to explain; it is also known and conceptualized in a distinctive way.

7. The Taxonomy of Responses: Type-A, Type-B, Type-C, Type-D, Type-E, Type-F

One of Chalmers’s most useful contributions is his taxonomy of possible responses to the consciousness problem. In “Consciousness and its Place in Nature,” he distinguishes reductive and nonreductive options, adapting and extending an earlier style of taxonomy associated with C. D. Broad.

The reductive or materialist options are:

Type-A materialism denies that there is any epistemic gap once the functional and behavioral facts are fully understood. On this view, once we explain access, report, discrimination, and other functions, there is no further problem.

Type-B materialism accepts an epistemic gap but denies that it implies an ontological gap. Consciousness is physical, but we cannot derive phenomenal truths from physical truths because of our concepts or modes of presentation.

Type-C materialism accepts that there is currently a deep explanatory gap but expects that future science or conceptual development will close it.

Chalmers rejects all three. He thinks Type-A materialism denies or deflates the phenomenon to be explained. Type-B materialism leaves primitive psychophysical identities unexplained. Type-C materialism postpones rather than solves the problem. He argues that if Type-B materialism treats identities between consciousness and physical states as primitive, it begins to resemble a nonreductive view with fundamental bridging principles rather than a genuinely reductive physicalism.

The main nonreductive options are:

Type-D dualism, or interactionism, holds that phenomenal properties are nonphysical and can causally influence the physical world.

Type-E dualism, or epiphenomenalism, holds that phenomenal properties are nonphysical but do not causally influence the physical world; physical processes give rise to experience, but experience does not affect physical processes.

Type-F monism, often associated with Russellian monism, panpsychism, or panprotopsychism, holds that consciousness or proto-consciousness is located in the intrinsic nature of the physical world. Physics describes structure and dynamics, but not the intrinsic categorical basis that realizes that structure.

Chalmers gives serious consideration to all three nonreductive options. He describes Type-D, Type-E, and Type-F as the best options among the available views, with Type-F especially attractive on grounds of integration and elegance, though it faces the serious combination problem.

This taxonomy is important because it shows that Chalmers’s philosophy is not reducible to simple “dualism.” He is firmly anti-reductive about consciousness, but he is more pluralistic about the best positive metaphysics. His early work is most closely associated with naturalistic property dualism. His later work gives increasing attention to Russellian monism, panpsychism, panprotopsychism, and idealism as possible ways to integrate consciousness into nature.

8. Naturalistic Dualism: Consciousness as Fundamental

The positive doctrine of The Conscious Mind is naturalistic dualism. It is dualist because consciousness is not reducible to physical properties. It is naturalistic because consciousness is still part of the natural order and governed by law.

The basic proposal is that a complete theory of the world may need fundamental psychophysical laws connecting physical processes and conscious experiences. Physics already posits fundamental entities and laws when reduction is unavailable. Chalmers suggests that consciousness may need to be treated similarly. The move is not from science to mysticism; it is from reductive science to a broader naturalism.

This is one reason Chalmers’s view differs from traditional Cartesian substance dualism. He does not primarily argue for immaterial souls or a separate mental substance. His central claim is that there are phenomenal properties not reducible to physical properties. These properties may lawfully correlate with physical or informational structures.

The early Chalmersian picture contains three especially important principles.

First, there is the principle of structural coherence: the structure of conscious experience is systematically correlated with the structure of awareness, where awareness is understood functionally. For example, differences and relations within perceptual experience correspond to functional discriminations and informational relations in cognition.

Second, there is the principle of organizational invariance: systems with the same fine-grained functional organization will have the same conscious experiences. This is developed through Chalmers’s absent qualia, fading qualia, and dancing qualia arguments. His consciousness papers describe these arguments as defenses of organizational invariance and nonreductive functionalism.

Third, there is the speculative idea of double-aspect information: information may have both a physical aspect and a phenomenal aspect. This idea is not meant as a complete theory, but as a candidate bridge principle. It allows Chalmers to connect consciousness with information-processing without reducing consciousness to information-processing.

The result is a hybrid position. Chalmers is anti-reductionist about phenomenal consciousness, but he is not anti-functionalist about the distribution of consciousness. Functional organization may not explain why consciousness exists, but it may lawfully determine which experiences occur.

9. Organizational Invariance, Functionalism, and Strong AI

Chalmers’s relation to functionalism is subtle. He rejects reductive functionalism about consciousness: consciousness cannot be identified with a functional role. But he accepts a nonreductive form of functionalism concerning the dependence of conscious experience on functional organization. If two systems share the same fine-grained causal organization, they will have the same experiences, even if one is biological and the other is silicon-based.

This is where Chalmers’s arguments about absent, fading, and dancing qualia become central. Very roughly, if consciousness could disappear or radically change while functional organization remained fixed, then a system might undergo massive changes in experience without any functional capacity to notice, report, or respond to those changes. Chalmers argues that such scenarios are implausible. Therefore, conscious experience is an organizational invariant.

This leads to Chalmers’s defense of strong artificial intelligence. If a machine has the right causal organization, it can have conscious experiences. The Conscious Mind explicitly includes a defense of strong AI, and Chalmers’s later work on computation analyzes the conditions under which physical systems implement computations and how computational structure can support mentality.

In “A Computational Foundation for the Study of Cognition,” Chalmers argues that computation is central to cognitive science, that implementation depends on a physical causal structure mirroring a formal computational structure, and that a minimal computationalism can be compatible with different views about the ultimate metaphysics of mind.

The key point is this: Chalmers does not think computation reductively explains consciousness. But he does think computational or causal organization may be sufficient for consciousness given the relevant psychophysical laws. A silicon system, a biological brain, or another physical system could all be conscious if they instantiate the right organization.

His recent work on large language models continues this line. In his 2023 paper “Could a Large Language Model be Conscious?,” Chalmers argues that current large language models face obstacles to consciousness, including lack of recurrent processing, global workspace architecture, and unified agency, but that successors overcoming such obstacles should be taken seriously as possible conscious systems.

10. Chalmers on Psychology and Cognitive Science

Chalmers’s philosophy of psychology is not a full empirical psychology. It is a framework for understanding what psychology can explain and where its limits lie.

In his framework, psychology studies the functional, behavioral, informational, and computational dimensions of mind. It explains how organisms discriminate stimuli, form beliefs, integrate information, attend, remember, deliberate, control action, and report internal states. These are the “easy problems,” but again only in the technical sense that they are functionally tractable.

Chalmers is therefore friendly to cognitive science. He treats computational explanation as a legitimate and powerful form of explanation for cognition. A cognitive theory explains a capacity by specifying the causal organization that produces it. Psychology and neuroscience can map these capacities onto brain mechanisms and computational architectures.

But Chalmers denies that this yields a complete theory of mind. Scientific psychology can explain awareness in the functional sense: access, availability, reportability, and behavioral control. It does not automatically explain consciousness in the phenomenal sense. That is why Chalmers insists on the distinction between the science of cognition and the science of consciousness.

His program for a science of consciousness therefore requires integration of third-person and first-person data. Chalmers’s consciousness papers describe this methodological task as connecting third-person data about brain and behavior with first-person data about experience.

This is crucial. Chalmers does not reject science because consciousness is first-personal. Rather, he thinks consciousness science must enlarge its data set. A mature science of consciousness would include:
1. third-person data from neuroscience, psychology, behavior, and computation;
2. first-person data from phenomenological report and experience sampling;
3. bridging principles connecting physical-functional structures with phenomenal structures;
4. theories of neural correlates that are constrained by both objective and subjective data.

Chalmers’s work on neural correlates of consciousness emphasizes that one must carefully distinguish different sorts of correlates and avoid conflating correlation with explanation. His consciousness papers include work on how to construct a science of consciousness, how to think about neural correlates, and how to connect first-person and third-person methods.

In this respect, Chalmers’s view is both scientifically optimistic and metaphysically demanding. Cognitive science can explain cognition. Neuroscience can discover correlates and mechanisms. But consciousness science must add psychophysical principles if it is to explain why experience accompanies those mechanisms.

11. The Meta-Problem of Consciousness

Chalmers’s later work on the meta-problem of consciousness deepens his relation to psychology. The hard problem asks why physical processes give rise to experience. The meta-problem asks why we think there is a hard problem. More specifically, it asks why humans make reports such as “consciousness is puzzling,” “experience seems nonphysical,” or “there is something over and above functional processing.”

Chalmers describes the meta-problem as the problem of explaining why we think consciousness is hard to explain. Unlike the hard problem, the meta-problem is itself an “easy” problem in the technical sense: it concerns behavior, cognition, judgment, and report, so it should be explainable by cognitive and neural mechanisms.

This is a major development in Chalmers’s philosophy of psychology. It acknowledges that our judgments about consciousness are psychological phenomena requiring explanation. Why do subjects form special concepts of experience? Why do they resist physicalist reduction? Why do they report an explanatory gap? Why do they distinguish experience from access, report, or behavior?

Chalmers argues that solving the meta-problem may illuminate or constrain solutions to the hard problem. It is not a replacement for the hard problem, because explaining why we say consciousness is puzzling does not by itself explain consciousness. But it may reveal the cognitive architecture that generates phenomenal concepts and hard-problem intuitions.

He also presents the meta-problem as an interdisciplinary research program involving psychology, neuroscience, artificial intelligence, linguistics, anthropology, experimental philosophy, and philosophy of mind.

This places Chalmers in an interesting relation to illusionism. Illusionists argue that consciousness, in the robust phenomenal sense, is an illusion produced by cognitive mechanisms. Chalmers accepts the importance of explaining the illusion-like or puzzlement-generating mechanisms, but he does not infer that consciousness itself is unreal. He treats the meta-problem as neutral between realism and illusionism. A promising account, on his view, may involve introspective models and concepts that represent experiences as primitive qualities in ways not transparently reducible to physical or functional descriptions.

The meta-problem therefore adds a psychological layer to Chalmers’s system. A complete theory of mind must explain not only consciousness and cognition, but also our cognition about consciousness.

12. Consciousness, Representation, and the Content of Experience

Chalmers also develops views about the representational character of experience. He is not a simple qualia theorist who treats experience as raw sensation without content. He is interested in how experiences present the world, how perceptual content is structured, and how phenomenal content relates to external objects, Fregean modes of presentation, and idealized “Edenic” perceptual contents.

His consciousness papers describe his view as a nonreductive, narrow, Fregean form of representationalism. That is important because many representationalists are reductive: they try to reduce phenomenal character to representational content. Chalmers resists that reduction. He can accept that experience has representational content while denying that phenomenality is exhausted by representation understood in physical, functional, or externalist terms.

The idea of Edenic content is especially revealing. Chalmers argues that perceptual experience often presents the world as having primitive qualitative properties — colors, sounds, textures — that may not exist in the external world exactly as presented. Ordinary perceptual experience presents an “Edenic” world: a world of perfect colors and qualities as they appear. The actual world may be more complex, involving physical properties that imperfectly satisfy or play the roles associated with these contents.

This connects philosophy of perception with the hard problem. Experience does not merely register external information; it presents a world under a phenomenal mode. A complete philosophy of mind must therefore address not only why experience exists, but also how experience represents.

13. Unity of Consciousness

Another important part of Chalmers’s philosophy is the unity of consciousness. Conscious experience is not normally a mere heap of independent qualia. It is integrated into a single field or subject-level perspective. Visual, auditory, bodily, emotional, and cognitive experiences are co-conscious: they occur together as parts of one conscious episode.

Chalmers has written on the unity of consciousness and on how unity connects to broader debates about experience and cognition. His consciousness papers list “The Unity of Consciousness” among his major works, alongside the absent/fading/dancing qualia arguments and his work on phenomenal concepts.

Unity matters for several reasons. It bears on personal identity, subjectivity, split-brain cases, attention, global workspace theory, and the combination problem for panpsychism. If micro-level conscious subjects or proto-conscious properties are supposed to combine into macro-consciousness, we need to explain how many small experiential elements become one unified subject. This is one reason the unity problem becomes central in Chalmers’s later engagement with panpsychism and Russellian monism.

14. Russellian Monism, Panpsychism, and Panprotopsychism

Chalmers’s later work gives increasing attention to Russellian monism, panpsychism, and panprotopsychism. The motivation is simple: standard physicalism seems unable to explain consciousness, but standard dualism faces causal and integration problems. Russellian views try to preserve the structural success of physics while adding that physics tells us only the relational, dispositional, or structural character of matter, not its intrinsic nature.

The thought is that consciousness, or proto-consciousness, might be located in the intrinsic nature of the physical. Physics describes what matter does; consciousness might reveal something about what matter is.

Chalmers characterizes panpsychism as the view that some fundamental physical entities have conscious experiences. He does not present this as obviously true, and he explicitly notes that he is not confident that panpsychism is true. Rather, he treats it as a serious theoretical option motivated by the failure of reductive materialism and by the causal difficulties facing more traditional dualism.

Panpsychism, in his “Hegelian” framing, can be understood as a synthesis between materialism and dualism. Materialism preserves the causal closure and continuity of nature but seems unable to account for experience. Dualism preserves the reality of experience but risks making consciousness causally isolated or metaphysically disconnected. Panpsychism tries to place experience inside nature from the start.

Panprotopsychism is a related but weaker view. It does not say that fundamental entities are conscious. It says they have proto-phenomenal properties that are not themselves experiences but can ground consciousness when appropriately structured.

The major obstacle is the combination problem. If tiny entities have tiny experiences, how do those experiences combine into the unified consciousness of a person? How do micro-subjects become a macro-subject? Chalmers treats this as a serious unresolved challenge for Type-F views.

Thus, Chalmers’s mature position is not simply “consciousness is fundamental, therefore dualism.” It is more nuanced: consciousness is not reducible to physical structure and function; the best metaphysical options include interactionist dualism, epiphenomenal property dualism, and Russellian/panpsychist monism; and future theory may decide among them by developing better psychophysical principles.

15. Causation, Epiphenomenalism, and the Paradox of Phenomenal Judgment

One of the deepest problems for Chalmers is mental causation. If consciousness is nonphysical, does it affect the physical world? If it does, then the causal closure of physics may be false. If it does not, then consciousness seems epiphenomenal: a causally inert byproduct.

Chalmers takes this problem seriously. Type-D dualism allows consciousness to affect physical processes, but at the cost of revising the causal closure of physics. Type-E dualism preserves physical causal closure, but treats phenomenal properties as causally inefficacious with respect to the physical. Chalmers’s taxonomy explicitly defines Type-D dualism as involving causal influence from phenomenal to physical properties and Type-E dualism as involving phenomenal properties that are ontologically distinct but do not affect the physical.

This leads to the paradox of phenomenal judgment. If consciousness is epiphenomenal, how can our judgments and reports about consciousness be caused by consciousness? Why do we talk about experience if experience has no causal influence?

Chalmers’s answer is subtle. He argues that there can be a systematic coherence between phenomenal facts and cognitive facts, even if the causal work at the physical level is done by physical processes. The physical system produces reports about consciousness, and the phenomenal properties lawfully correspond to the physical-functional states that produce those reports. This is not fully satisfying to many critics, but it is part of the price Chalmers is willing to consider if one accepts both nonreductive consciousness and physical causal closure.

The later meta-problem helps here. It turns the production of phenomenal judgments into a research problem: explain the mechanisms that lead beings like us to judge that consciousness exists and is hard to explain. But the meta-problem does not remove the metaphysical tension. It clarifies it.

16. Extended Mind and Extended Cognition

Chalmers’s philosophy of mind also includes his influential collaboration with Andy Clark on the extended mind. Their 1998 paper “The Extended Mind” argues for active externalism: the view that cognitive processes can extend beyond the biological organism when external tools or structures play the right active role in cognition. The paper asks where the mind stops and proposes that the environment can be part of the cognitive process, not merely an input to it.

This is a striking complement to Chalmers’s anti-reductionism about consciousness. On cognition, he is liberal and externalist: cognitive processes and even some mental states may partly depend on notebooks, computers, maps, or other environmental structures when they function as integrated parts of the cognitive system. On consciousness, however, he is more cautious.

In later work, Chalmers distinguishes extended cognition from extended consciousness. He accepts that cognitive processes and some mental states may be partly constituted by external entities when those entities play the right role. But he and Clark were more skeptical that consciousness itself extends outside the head or body in the same way. Chalmers notes that belief can be mediated by external availability, whereas consciousness seems more constrained by immediate experiential integration.

This distinction is important for reconstructing his philosophy. Chalmers is not a narrow internalist about all mind. He is willing to extend cognition beyond the organism. But he does not simply extend phenomenal consciousness wherever cognition extends. Again, cognition and consciousness must be distinguished.

17. Virtual Reality, Simulation, and the Ontology of Mind

Chalmers’s later book Reality+ extends his philosophy of mind into virtual reality and simulation metaphysics. The central thesis is that virtual reality can be genuine reality: virtual objects can be real objects, virtual lives can be meaningful, and living in a simulation would not make one’s world unreal.

This is not merely a side project. It connects to Chalmers’s philosophy of mind in several ways.

First, it reinforces substrate independence. If minds can exist in computational or simulated environments, then biological embodiment is not metaphysically necessary for mentality.

Second, it connects to skepticism. The possibility that we are in a simulation resembles Cartesian skeptical scenarios, but Chalmers argues that such a possibility need not undermine the reality of our world. If our tables are constituted by digital processes, they may still be real tables.

Third, it raises ethical questions. If virtual agents or artificial systems are conscious, they may have moral status. Chalmers’s recent work explicitly includes AI welfare and the philosophy of language models as part of his research trajectory.

Fourth, it expands the philosophy of mind into the philosophy of technology. Minds may be biological, artificial, extended, embedded, or virtual. The crucial issue is not the material substrate but the organization, experience, and lawlike connections between the physical/computational and the phenomenal.

18. Chalmers’s Method: A Priori Analysis, Science, and First-Person Data

Chalmers’s method is distinctive because it combines several styles of philosophy that are often kept apart.

He uses a priori analysis: thought experiments, modal reasoning, conceivability arguments, two-dimensional semantics, and scrutability. His book Constructing the World extends Carnap’s project and defends a scrutability thesis according to which, in principle, ideal reasoning from a limited base of truths can determine all truths.

He uses scientific respectability: he engages cognitive science, neuroscience, AI, computation, and theories of neural correlates.

He uses phenomenology in a disciplined but not purely Husserlian sense: first-person experience is data that must be included in the theory.

He uses metaphysical system-building: he asks what the world must be like if consciousness is real and not reducible.

This makes Chalmers a philosopher of mind in the grand systematic tradition. He is not only analyzing words or intuitions. He is trying to determine the place of consciousness in nature.

19. The Main Criticisms of Chalmers

Any rigorous reconstruction must include the main objections.

The first objection is illusionism or eliminativism. Critics argue that Chalmers reifies a problematic notion of phenomenal consciousness. Once we explain access, report, introspection, memory, attention, and self-modeling, nothing remains. Chalmers’s reply is that this denies the datum: experience itself. The hard problem arises because experience is not equivalent to any of those functions.

The second objection is the phenomenal concept strategy. Type-B physicalists argue that the epistemic gap is due to special phenomenal concepts, not to nonphysical properties. Chalmers replies that unless these concepts can be explained in a way that also explains their phenomenal reference, the strategy fails. If identities between phenomenal and physical states are taken as primitive, they look more like brute psychophysical laws than reductions.

The third objection targets the conceivability-possibility move. Even if zombies are conceivable, perhaps they are not metaphysically possible. Chalmers’s two-dimensional semantics is designed to answer this, but many philosophers remain unconvinced.

The fourth objection concerns causal exclusion. If consciousness is nonphysical, how can it affect the physical world without overdetermination or violation of physical closure? Chalmers acknowledges this pressure and treats Type-D, Type-E, and Type-F views as competing responses rather than pretending the problem is easy.

The fifth objection concerns panpsychism and combination. If consciousness is fundamental at the micro-level, how do micro-experiences combine into unified macro-experience? Chalmers recognizes this as one of the most serious problems for panpsychism and panprotopsychism.

The sixth objection is methodological. Some argue that first-person reports are too unreliable to ground a science. Chalmers’s response is not to treat introspection as infallible, but to treat experience as data that must be methodologically refined and correlated with third-person evidence.

These criticisms are not peripheral. They define the live battleground around Chalmers’s system.

20. Extracting a Comprehensive Philosophy of Mind from Chalmers

A comprehensive Chalmersian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as the following system.

20.1 Ontology

Reality contains physical structure and dynamics, but physical structure and dynamics do not exhaust reality. Conscious experience is a real feature of the world. It is not reducible to behavioral, functional, neural, or computational facts.

The precise positive ontology remains open among several nonreductive options. The early Chalmersian view is naturalistic property dualism: phenomenal properties are fundamental and connected to physical properties by psychophysical laws. The later Chalmersian space includes Russellian monism, panpsychism, panprotopsychism, and idealism as serious alternatives.

20.2 Mind Has Two Aspects

Mind has a psychological aspect and a phenomenal aspect.

The psychological aspect concerns cognition, information-processing, rationality, perception, memory, belief, desire, attention, language, agency, and behavior.

The phenomenal aspect concerns subjective experience: what it is like to see, feel, think, imagine, suffer, enjoy, or be a subject.

A theory that explains only psychological function is incomplete. A theory that ignores cognitive science is also incomplete.

20.3 Psychology Is Functionally Explainable

Scientific psychology and cognitive science explain mental capacities by identifying mechanisms, computations, representations, causal roles, neural systems, and behavioral dispositions. This is legitimate and powerful.

The mind as studied by psychology is largely the mind as causal-functional organization. Chalmers is therefore compatible with computational cognitive science, artificial intelligence research, and functional explanation.

20.4 Consciousness Requires Psychophysical Principles

Because phenomenal truths are not entailed by physical-functional truths, a complete theory needs principles connecting physical or informational structures with experience. These may be fundamental laws, just as basic physical laws are fundamental.

The most important candidate principles are structural coherence, organizational invariance, and perhaps double-aspect information.

20.5 Substrate Independence Is Plausible

If consciousness depends on fine-grained functional organization rather than biological material as such, then artificial systems can be conscious in principle. This supports strong AI and makes machine consciousness a serious possibility.

Chalmers does not claim all computers are conscious. The system must instantiate the right organization and satisfy the relevant psychophysical laws. But biological carbon is not sacred.

20.6 Cognition May Be Extended

Cognitive processes can sometimes extend into the environment. Tools, notebooks, digital devices, and external representations may become parts of cognitive systems when they play the right causal role.

But extended cognition does not automatically entail extended consciousness. Phenomenal consciousness may require a tighter unity or integration than belief, memory, or problem-solving.

20.7 First-Person Data Are Indispensable

A science of consciousness cannot rely only on external observation. It must incorporate first-person data about experience, while treating such data critically and systematically.

The correct method is neither pure introspection nor pure third-person objectivism. It is a psychophysical science linking subjective reports, phenomenological structure, neural correlates, cognitive function, and formal theory.

20.8 Phenomenal Concepts Are Special

Our concepts of experience are not ordinary functional or physical concepts. They are tied to the first-person presentation of experience. This explains why consciousness generates distinctive epistemological puzzles, including the knowledge argument and explanatory gap.

But the specialness of phenomenal concepts does not, for Chalmers, dissolve the metaphysical problem. It helps articulate it.

20.9 The Meta-Problem Is a Necessary Subproblem

A complete philosophy of mind must explain why conscious beings judge consciousness to be puzzling. This is a psychological and cognitive-scientific problem. Solving it may constrain theories of consciousness, but it does not by itself solve the hard problem.

20.10 Ethics Follows from Consciousness

Although Chalmers is not primarily a moral philosopher of mind, his system has ethical implications. If consciousness is substrate-independent and artificial or virtual beings can be conscious, then AI welfare, virtual suffering, and the moral status of simulated persons become serious philosophical and practical issues.

21. Final Assessment

Chalmers’s philosophy of mind is best described as naturalistic psychophysical realism. It is realist because consciousness is not dismissed, deflated, or reduced away. It is psychophysical because it seeks lawful connections between physical-functional organization and phenomenal experience. It is naturalistic because consciousness is treated as part of the world, not as a supernatural intrusion. It is anti-reductive because consciousness is not derivable from physical structure and dynamics alone.

His historical importance lies in forcing philosophy of mind to confront a distinction that functionalism and cognitive science often blurred: the distinction between explaining what minds do and explaining why mental life is experienced from within. After Chalmers, any serious theory of mind must say whether it denies the hard problem, reduces it, explains it through new laws, grounds it in the intrinsic nature of matter, or dissolves it by explaining our judgments about it.

The Chalmersian system is not a finished metaphysics. It is a research program. Its central commitments are stable: phenomenal consciousness is real; reductive physicalism fails; cognitive science explains function; consciousness science must connect first-person and third-person data; artificial consciousness is possible in principle; and the ultimate theory of mind will require principles deeper than current physical-functional explanation. Its unresolved choice is among naturalistic dualism, epiphenomenalism, interactionism, Russellian monism, panpsychism, panprotopsychism, and related views.

That is precisely why Chalmers remains central. His work does not merely offer one more theory of consciousness. It reorganizes the field around the question any comprehensive philosophy of mind must answer: how can a world described in structural, causal, and functional terms also be a world with subjective experience?

PreviousDavid Rosenthal NextBernard Baars

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
